title: Cardiovascular Collapse Cardiac Arrest and Sudden Cardiac Death
authors: Christine M. Albert, William H. Sauer

items.subtopic: Cardiovascular Collapse, Cardiac Arrest, and Sudden Cardiac Death | OVERVIEW AND DEFINITIONS (SEE TABLE 317-1)
items.title: Understanding the Spectrum of Cardiovascular Collapse to Sudden Cardiac Death
items.content: Cardiovascular collapse involves severe hypotension due to acute cardiac dysfunction or loss of peripheral vasculature resistance, leading to cerebral hypoperfusion and unconsciousness. If not resolved spontaneously, it progresses to cardiac arrest, which can result in sudden cardiac death (SCD) if resuscitation fails. SCD includes both witnessed rapid deaths presumed to be arrhythmic and unexplained rapid deaths where resuscitation was not attempted.
keyPoints: Cardiovascular collapse results from acute cardiac dysfunction or loss of peripheral vasculature resistance, leading to cerebral hypoperfusion.
SCA requires emergency intervention for restoration of circulation; less than 10% survive to hospital discharge despite EMS attempts.
SCD includes both witnessed and unwitnessed rapid deaths presumed due to cardiac causes within 1 hour of symptom onset or in association with a collapse.
Approximately half of all SCDs are not witnessed, complicating the determination of cause without autopsy.
items.reference: Cardiovascular Collapse Cardiac Arrest and Sudden Cardiac Death · Cardiovascular Collapse, Cardiac Arrest, and Sudden Cardiac Death | OVERVIEW AND DEFINITIONS (SEE TABLE 317-1)

items.subtopic: Cardiovascular Collapse, Cardiac Arrest, and Sudden Cardiac Death | OVERVIEW AND DEFINITIONS (SEE TABLE 317-1)
items.title: Epidemiology and Challenges in Defining Sudden Cardiac Death
items.content: Sudden cardiac death is a significant public health issue with limited surveillance. It encompasses both witnessed sudden deaths presumed to be arrhythmic and unexplained rapid deaths without resuscitation attempts. The lack of autopsies and national reporting systems complicates the accurate estimation of its incidence and causes, especially in unwitnessed cases where noncardiac conditions may contribute.
keyPoints: SCD is defined as an unexpected death within 1 hour of symptom onset or during a witnessed collapse, presumed to be cardiac in origin.
In the U.S., few deaths undergo autopsy, making it difficult to exclude rapid noncardiac causes like acute cerebral hemorrhage.
Most countries lack national surveillance for SCD, limiting precise incidence and mechanism data.
Unwitnessed SCD cases may include a significant percentage of noncardiac causes due to expanded definitions.
items.reference: Cardiovascular Collapse Cardiac Arrest and Sudden Cardiac Death · Cardiovascular Collapse, Cardiac Arrest, and Sudden Cardiac Death | OVERVIEW AND DEFINITIONS (SEE TABLE 317-1)

items.title: Incidence and Impact of Sudden Cardiac Death (SCD) Across Demographics
items.content: Sudden cardiac death (SCD) is a significant public health issue, accounting for 50% of all cardiac deaths. It affects different demographics variably based on age, gender, race, and geographic region.
keyPoints: SCD accounts for 15% of all deaths and 50% of all cardiac deaths.
Estimated annual SCDs in the U.S.: 210,000.
Societal burden: 2 million years of potential life lost for men; 1.3 million for women.
SCD rates are lower in Asia (52.5 per 100,000 person-years) compared to Europe (86.4), North America (98.1), and Australia (111.9).
SCD is rare under age 35 (1–3 per 100,000 per year) but increases with age.
Women have a lower incidence of SCD than men; more likely to present with pulseless electrical activity (PEA).
Black Americans have higher rates of SCD and are more likely to be found with PEA compared to white Americans.
Hispanic ethnicity is associated with lower rates of SCD despite higher cardiac risk factors.
items.reference: Cardiovascular Collapse Cardiac Arrest and Sudden Cardiac Death · DEMOGRAPHICS

items.title: Factors Influencing Survival Rates in Sudden Cardiac Death (SCD)
items.content: Survival rates from sudden cardiac death vary significantly by gender and race, influenced by differences in presentation and socioeconomic factors.
keyPoints: Women are more likely to have SCD occur at home compared to men.
Black Americans have lower survival rates post-cardiac arrest than white Americans.
Socioeconomic disparities contribute to resuscitation likelihood but do not fully explain racial differences in SCD rates.
The decline in SCD rate has been less pronounced for younger women compared to men.
items.reference: Cardiovascular Collapse Cardiac Arrest and Sudden Cardiac Death · DEMOGRAPHICS

items.title: Elevated SCD Risk in Patients with Preexisting Cardiac Conditions
items.content: Patients with preexisting coronary heart disease (CHD) and heart failure (HF) have a significantly elevated risk of sudden cardiac death (SCD), with a four- to tenfold increase. This highlights the importance of managing these conditions aggressively to mitigate SCD risk.
keyPoints: Preexisting CHD and HF are associated with a four- to tenfold increase in SCD risk.
Risk factors for SCD overlap significantly with those for CHD and heart failure, including hypertension, diabetes, hypercholesterolemia, obesity, and smoking.
Diabetes is a particularly strong risk factor for SCD even in patients with established CHD.
items.reference: Cardiovascular Collapse Cardiac Arrest and Sudden Cardiac Death · RISK FACTORS (SEE FIG. 317-1)

items.title: Impact of Lifestyle and Genetic Factors on SCD Risk
items.content: Lifestyle modifications such as smoking cessation can significantly reduce the risk of sudden cardiac death, particularly in individuals without overt CHD. Additionally, a genetic predisposition to ventricular arrhythmias may increase SCD risk independently of coronary heart disease.
keyPoints: Smoking markedly elevates SCD risk; cessation lowers risk especially before overt CHD develops.
A history of SCD in a first-degree relative increases the risk for fatal ventricular arrhythmia, particularly during acute myocardial infarction (MI).
Genetic factors may predispose individuals to fatal ventricular arrhythmias in ischemic settings rather than general coronary heart disease.
items.reference: Cardiovascular Collapse Cardiac Arrest and Sudden Cardiac Death · RISK FACTORS (SEE FIG. 317-1)

items.title: Circadian and Seasonal Variations in SCD/SCA Incidence
items.content: Sudden cardiac death (SCD) and sudden cardiac arrest (SCA) exhibit circadian and seasonal variations, with higher incidence rates during morning hours, later afternoon, winter in the northern hemisphere, and summer in the southern hemisphere. These patterns may be influenced by environmental factors such as temperature and light exposure.
keyPoints: Circadian peaks: Morning hours and later afternoon
Seasonal variability: Higher in winter (northern hemisphere) and summer (southern hemisphere)
Environmental influences: Temperature, light exposure
items.reference: Cardiovascular Collapse Cardiac Arrest and Sudden Cardiac Death · PRECIPITATING FACTORS | UNDERLYING HEART DISEASE (FIG. 317-1)

items.title: Role of Underlying Heart Disease in SCD
items.content: Coronary artery disease (CAD) is the most common cause of sudden cardiac death, with prevalence varying by age, race, and sex. Approximately 70% of SCDs in white men are due to CAD, compared to 40-50% in women and blacks. Nonischemic cardiomyopathies also significantly contribute to SCD.
keyPoints: Most common cause: Coronary artery disease (CAD)
Prevalence by demographics: ~70% in white men; 40–50% in women and blacks
Other significant causes: Nonischemic cardiomyopathies
items.reference: Cardiovascular Collapse Cardiac Arrest and Sudden Cardiac Death · PRECIPITATING FACTORS | UNDERLYING HEART DISEASE (FIG. 317-1)

items.title: Importance of Initial Rhythm in Out-of-Hospital Cardiac Arrest
items.content: The initial rhythm identified by EMS upon arrival at an out-of-hospital cardiac arrest scene is crucial for determining the potential cause and prognosis. Historically, ventricular fibrillation (VF) was common, but its incidence has decreased to 20–25%, with pulseless electrical activity (PEA) and asystole now more prevalent.
keyPoints: Initial rhythm indicates potential cause and prognosis of cardiac arrest.
Historical VF incidence: over half in early EMS systems.
Current VF incidence: 20–25%.
Most common current scenarios: PEA and asystole.
VF still predominant in public locations with short time to EMS arrival.
Decrease in VF attributed to lower CHD incidence, beta blockers use, and ICDs.
items.reference: Cardiovascular Collapse Cardiac Arrest and Sudden Cardiac Death · CARDIAC RHYTHMS AND SUDDEN DEATH

items.title: Increasing Incidence of PEA in Cardiac Arrests
items.content: There is an observed increase in the incidence of pulseless electrical activity (PEA) in cardiac arrests, suggesting a rise in sudden cardiac death due to abrupt hemodynamic collapse without preceding fatal arrhythmia. This trend may be influenced by an aging population and higher prevalence of severe comorbidities.
keyPoints: Increase in PEA incidence over recent years.
PEA suggests SCD from abrupt hemodynamic collapse without prior arrhythmia.
Possible explanations: aging population, increased end-stage cardiovascular disease.
Older patients more likely to have arrests at home with acute precipitants like respiratory or metabolic issues.
items.reference: Cardiovascular Collapse Cardiac Arrest and Sudden Cardiac Death · CARDIAC RHYTHMS AND SUDDEN DEATH

items.title: Mechanisms of SCD in CAD and Timing Post-MI
items.content: SCD in coronary artery disease (CAD) is primarily due to acute myocardial infarction or transient ischemia leading to polymorphic VT and VF. The risk of SCD increases up to 10-fold after an MI, with the highest rates within the first 30 days. Early post-MI mechanisms include nonarrhythmic causes like myocardial rupture, while later mechanisms involve ischemic VT and scar-related arrhythmias.
keyPoints: Acute MI or transient ischemia leads to polymorphic VT and VF.
Risk of SCD increases up to 10-fold after an MI.
Highest absolute rates of SCD occur within the first 30 days post-MI.
Early mechanisms: myocardial rupture, extensive reinfarction.
Later mechanisms: ischemic VT, scar-related ventricular arrhythmias.
items.reference: Cardiovascular Collapse Cardiac Arrest and Sudden Cardiac Death · DISEASE-SPECIFIC MECHANISMS

items.title: SCD in Nonischemic Cardiomyopathies and Absence of Structural Heart Disease
items.content: In nonischemic cardiomyopathies, scar-mediated reentrant VT can occur due to fibrosis or inflammatory infiltrates. In congenital heart disease, surgical scars may facilitate ventricular reentry. Without structural heart disease, VF can result from inherited ion channel abnormalities (e.g., long QT syndrome), rapid atrial fibrillation with WPW syndrome, drug toxicities prolonging the QT interval, and blunt chest impact causing commotio cordis.
keyPoints: Nonischemic cardiomyopathies: fibrosis/inflammatory infiltrates cause reentrant VT.
Congenital heart disease: surgical scars can lead to ventricular reentry.
Absence of structural heart disease: VF from ion channel abnormalities, WPW syndrome, drug toxicities, commotio cordis.
PEA causes include pulmonary emboli, exsanguination, respiratory arrest.
items.reference: Cardiovascular Collapse Cardiac Arrest and Sudden Cardiac Death · DISEASE-SPECIFIC MECHANISMS

items.title: Importance of Rapid Response in Out-of-Hospital Cardiac Arrest
items.content: The rapid identification and initiation of resuscitation measures by lay rescuers are crucial for improving survival rates in out-of-hospital cardiac arrest (OHCA). The 'out-of-hospital chain of survival' emphasizes early activation of emergency response, prompt CPR with chest compressions, quick defibrillation, advanced life support, postcardiac arrest care, and recovery.
keyPoints: Survival rates to hospital discharge for OHCA can approach 30% in patients found in VT or VF.
Overall survival rates are higher for patients receiving CPR, with recent studies reporting rates of 16%.
Socioeconomic disparities affect the administration of CPR and AED use, impacting survival rates among black and Hispanic populations.
Time to initiation of resuscitation is critical; success decreases markedly after 5 minutes.
Survival outcomes are best for VT, worse for VF, and poor for PEA and asystole.
items.reference: Cardiovascular Collapse Cardiac Arrest and Sudden Cardiac Death · MANAGEMENT OF CARDIAC ARREST

items.title: Factors Influencing Outcomes in Cardiac Arrest Resuscitation
items.content: The success of resuscitation efforts is influenced by the time to initiation, rhythm at EMT arrival, and patient factors such as age, clinical state, and comorbidities. Early defibrillation and effective CPR are key components that improve survival chances.
keyPoints: Survival rates decrease significantly after 5 minutes from collapse without resuscitation.
Rhythm at the time of EMT arrival affects outcomes: best for VT, worse for VF, poor for PEA and asystole.
Patient factors such as age, clinical state, and comorbidities prior to arrest influence survival outcomes.
items.reference: Cardiovascular Collapse Cardiac Arrest and Sudden Cardiac Death · MANAGEMENT OF CARDIAC ARREST

items.subtopic: INITIAL EVALUATION AND INITIATION OF CPR | RHYTHM-BASED MANAGEMENT (SEE FIG. 317-2)
items.title: Initiation of CPR: Timing and Technique
items.content: CPR should be initiated promptly after confirming the absence of a pulse, with chest compressions at a rate of 100–120/min and depth of 5 cm to ensure effective cardiac output.
keyPoints: Check for response, call emergency services immediately.
Assess pulse within 10 seconds; if absent, start CPR.
Chest compressions: 100–120/min, 5 cm depth, full recoil.
Minimize interruptions to reduce end-organ ischemia.
Hands-only CPR is effective for lay rescuers.
items.reference: Cardiovascular Collapse Cardiac Arrest and Sudden Cardiac Death · INITIAL EVALUATION AND INITIATION OF CPR | RHYTHM-BASED MANAGEMENT (SEE FIG. 317-2)

items.subtopic: INITIAL EVALUATION AND INITIATION OF CPR | RHYTHM-BASED MANAGEMENT (SEE FIG. 317-2)
items.title: Rapid Defibrillation and AED Use
items.content: Defibrillation should be performed as quickly as possible using an AED, which is user-friendly for both lay rescuers and trained personnel.
keyPoints: AEDs improve survival rates in witnessed arrests.
Apply AED patches and pause compressions briefly to analyze rhythm.
Deliver 200-J biphasic shock if VF or VT is detected.
Resume compressions immediately after defibrillation.
items.reference: Cardiovascular Collapse Cardiac Arrest and Sudden Cardiac Death · INITIAL EVALUATION AND INITIATION OF CPR | RHYTHM-BASED MANAGEMENT (SEE FIG. 317-2)

items.subtopic: INITIAL EVALUATION AND INITIATION OF CPR | RHYTHM-BASED MANAGEMENT (SEE FIG. 317-2)
items.title: Advanced Cardiac Life Support and Medication Administration
items.content: Upon availability of advanced cardiac life support, establish IV or intraosseous access for medication administration, including epinephrine every 3–5 minutes.
keyPoints: Establish IV/intraosseous line for drug delivery.
Administer epinephrine 1 mg every 3–5 min intravenously/intraosseously.
Use amiodarone or lidocaine if VT/VF recurs after defibrillation.
Consider etiology-specific therapies like magnesium for torsades des pointes.
items.reference: Cardiovascular Collapse Cardiac Arrest and Sudden Cardiac Death · INITIAL EVALUATION AND INITIATION OF CPR | RHYTHM-BASED MANAGEMENT (SEE FIG. 317-2)

items.title: When to Perform Emergent Angiography in Post-Cardiac Arrest Patients
items.content: Emergent coronary angiography is recommended for patients with ST-segment elevation indicative of acute MI, or if they exhibit cardiogenic shock, electrical instability, significant myocardial damage, or ongoing ischemia. However, it may not improve outcomes compared to delayed angiography in out-of-hospital cardiac arrest cases without ST-segment elevation.
keyPoints: ST-segment elevation indicates potential acute MI and warrants emergent coronary angiography with percutaneous angioplasty and stenting.
Consider emergent angiography for cardiogenic shock, electrical instability, significant myocardial damage, or ongoing ischemia.
Emergent angiography does not improve outcomes in out-of-hospital cardiac arrest without ST-segment elevation.
items.reference: Cardiovascular Collapse Cardiac Arrest and Sudden Cardiac Death · POSTCARDIAC ARREST ACUTE MANAGEMENT

items.title: Optimizing Hemodynamics and Identifying Reversible Causes Post-Cardiac Arrest
items.content: Post-resuscitation management should focus on maintaining systolic blood pressure >90 mmHg, mean blood pressure >65 mmHg, and addressing reversible causes like hyperkalemia, severe hypokalemia, or drug toxicity with QT prolongation.
keyPoints: Maintain systolic BP >90 mmHg and mean BP >65 mmHg post-resuscitation.
Identify and treat potentially reversible causes such as hyperkalemia, severe hypokalemia, and drug toxicity causing torsades de pointes.
items.reference: Cardiovascular Collapse Cardiac Arrest and Sudden Cardiac Death · POSTCARDIAC ARREST ACUTE MANAGEMENT

items.title: Therapeutic Hypothermia for Neurologic Recovery in Cardiac Arrest Patients
items.content: Targeted temperature management (32–37.5°C) is recommended for comatose cardiac arrest patients to improve survival and neurologic recovery, particularly those with shockable rhythms or lack of purposeful response post-ROSC.
keyPoints: Therapeutic hypothermia improves survival and neurologic outcomes in comatose cardiac arrest patients.
Target temperature: 32–37.5°C for at least 24 hours.
No benefit from pre-hospital induction of hypothermia; avoid large volumes of cold saline to prevent pulmonary edema.
items.reference: Cardiovascular Collapse Cardiac Arrest and Sudden Cardiac Death · POSTCARDIAC ARREST ACUTE MANAGEMENT

items.subtopic: LONG-TERM MANAGEMENT AFTER SURVIVAL OF OUT-OF-HOSPITAL CARDIAC ARREST
items.title: Guidelines for ICD Implantation in Post-Cardiac Arrest Patients
items.content: For patients who survive out-of-hospital cardiac arrest with shockable rhythms (VF or VT) and no transient reversible cause, an ICD is recommended for secondary prevention of SCA/SCD. However, if VF occurs within the first 48 hours post-MI without a large infarction or significantly depressed LVEF (<35%), immediate ICD implantation may not be necessary due to similar sudden death risk as non-arrest MI survivors.
keyPoints: ICD recommended for shockable rhythms (VF, VT) with reasonable life expectancy and no transient cause.
VF within 48 hours post-MI does not always require ICD unless LVEF <35% or large infarction is present.
Early ICD implantation post-large MI does not improve overall survival due to non-arrhythmic sudden deaths (ischemia, rupture).
Wearable defibrillator may be used temporarily for patients with large infarcts during left ventricular remodeling.
items.reference: Cardiovascular Collapse Cardiac Arrest and Sudden Cardiac Death · LONG-TERM MANAGEMENT AFTER SURVIVAL OF OUT-OF-HOSPITAL CARDIAC ARREST

items.subtopic: LONG-TERM MANAGEMENT AFTER SURVIVAL OF OUT-OF-HOSPITAL CARDIAC ARREST
items.title: Evaluating Cardiac Arrest Causes and Management Strategies
items.content: Post-cardiac arrest evaluation should focus on identifying underlying causes, such as structural heart disease or primary electrical diseases. In cases of suspected inflammatory cardiomyopathy, cardiac PET scan or endomyocardial biopsy may be necessary. For reversible causes like hyperkalemia or drug-induced torsades des pointes, ICD is generally not needed.
keyPoints: Full evaluation for structural heart disease includes CAD assessment, echocardiography/MRI, and LVEF measurement.
Cardiac PET scan/endomyocardial biopsy for suspected inflammatory cardiomyopathy (e.g., sarcoidosis, myocarditis).
Evaluation for primary electrical diseases if no structural abnormalities are found.
ICD not usually needed for reversible causes like hyperkalemia or drug toxicity with torsades des pointes.
items.reference: Cardiovascular Collapse Cardiac Arrest and Sudden Cardiac Death · LONG-TERM MANAGEMENT AFTER SURVIVAL OF OUT-OF-HOSPITAL CARDIAC ARREST

items.subtopic: LONG-TERM MANAGEMENT AFTER SURVIVAL OF OUT-OF-HOSPITAL CARDIAC ARREST
items.title: Psychological and Rehabilitation Needs Post-Cardiac Arrest
items.content: Addressing the psychological needs of both cardiac arrest survivors and their families is crucial. Comprehensive rehabilitation plans should be developed to manage physical, neurologic, cardiopulmonary, and cognitive impairments before hospital discharge.
keyPoints: Assess and address psychological needs of SCA survivor and family members.
Develop comprehensive rehabilitation plans for physical, neurologic, cardiopulmonary, and cognitive impairments.
items.reference: Cardiovascular Collapse Cardiac Arrest and Sudden Cardiac Death · LONG-TERM MANAGEMENT AFTER SURVIVAL OF OUT-OF-HOSPITAL CARDIAC ARREST

items.subtopic: PREVENTION OF SCD | SCD RISK STRATIFICATION
items.title: Importance of Preventive Measures in Reducing Cardiac Arrest Mortality
items.content: Despite improvements in CPR and postresuscitation care, the majority of cardiac arrest patients do not survive to hospital discharge. Many arrests occur at home with longer EMS response times and are often unwitnessed, reducing the likelihood of effective resuscitation. Preventive strategies are essential for lowering mortality rates from cardiac arrest.
keyPoints: 90% of cardiac arrest patients will not survive to be discharged from the hospital.
~20% of survivors have severe neurologic or physical disability.
50% of cardiac arrests are unwitnessed, limiting resuscitation efforts.
items.reference: Cardiovascular Collapse Cardiac Arrest and Sudden Cardiac Death · PREVENTION OF SCD | SCD RISK STRATIFICATION

items.subtopic: PREVENTION OF SCD | SCD RISK STRATIFICATION
items.title: SCD Risk Stratification in Structural Heart Disease
items.content: In patients with structural heart disease, the risk of sudden cardiac death (SCD) is linked to left ventricular function. Post-myocardial infarction (MI), the risk of SCD increases as left ventricular ejection fraction (LVEF) decreases, particularly below 40%. Other markers include unexplained syncope, sustained ventricular tachycardia (VT) at electrophysiologic study, and QRS prolongation. However, these tests generally predict cardiovascular death broadly without distinguishing between arrhythmic and non-arrhythmic causes.
keyPoints: Depressed left ventricular function is a key marker for SCD risk in structural heart disease.
SCD risk increases gradually as LVEF decreases to 40% and exponentially thereafter post-MI.
Sustained VT at EP study identifies higher SCA risk but has low sensitivity with significantly reduced LV function.
items.reference: Cardiovascular Collapse Cardiac Arrest and Sudden Cardiac Death · PREVENTION OF SCD | SCD RISK STRATIFICATION

items.subtopic: ☑ PREVENTIVE THERAPIES FOR SCD IN HIGH-RISK POPULATIONS
items.title: Role of Beta-Adrenergic Blockers and Other Medications in Reducing SCD Risk
items.content: Beta-adrenergic blockers are effective in reducing the risk of sudden cardiac death (SCD) across various conditions, including post-myocardial infarction (MI), ischemic and nonischemic cardiomyopathy, and long QT syndrome. Other medications like angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitors, aldosterone antagonists, angiotensin receptor/neprilysin inhibitors, and SGLT2 inhibitors also contribute to lowering SCD risk in patients with structural heart disease.
keyPoints: Beta-adrenergic blockers reduce SCD risk post-MI, in ischemic and nonischemic cardiomyopathy, and LQTS.
ACE inhibitors, aldosterone antagonists, ARNI, and SGLT2i are associated with reduced SCD in structural heart disease.
items.reference: Cardiovascular Collapse Cardiac Arrest and Sudden Cardiac Death · ☑ PREVENTIVE THERAPIES FOR SCD IN HIGH-RISK POPULATIONS

items.subtopic: ☑ PREVENTIVE THERAPIES FOR SCD IN HIGH-RISK POPULATIONS
items.title: Indications for ICD Implantation in Primary Prevention of Sudden Death
items.content: Implantable cardioverter-defibrillators (ICDs) are recommended for primary prevention of sudden death in patients with a substantial risk of sustained ventricular tachycardia or fibrillation despite optimal medical therapy. Indications vary based on the underlying heart disease and its severity, with specific criteria for post-MI patients, such as those with NYHA class II–III heart failure and LVEF <35%, or NYHA class I with LVEF <30%. ICDs are not beneficial within 40 days of an MI but are indicated in other high-risk scenarios.
keyPoints: ICDs recommended for primary prevention in patients at risk of sustained VT/VF on optimal therapy.
Indications include post-MI >40 days with NYHA II–III HF and LVEF <35%, or NYHA I with LVEF <30%.
ICDs not beneficial within 40 days post-MI; reassessment after 90 days of myocardial revascularization.
items.reference: Cardiovascular Collapse Cardiac Arrest and Sudden Cardiac Death · ☑ PREVENTIVE THERAPIES FOR SCD IN HIGH-RISK POPULATIONS

items.subtopic: TABLE 317-4 Implantable Cardioverter Defibrillator (ICD) Not Indicated | THE CHALLENGE OF SCD PREVENTION (FIG. 317-3)
items.title: When ICD is not indicated despite potential SCD risk
items.content: An ICD should not be implanted in patients who do not have a reasonable expectation of survival with an acceptable functional status for at least one year, even if they meet the criteria for implantation. This includes those with severe cardiomyopathy where competing causes of mortality are significant.
keyPoints: ICD is not indicated when there's no reasonable expectation of 1-year survival with acceptable functional status.
Competing causes of mortality must be considered, especially in patients with severe cardiomyopathy.
The DANISH trial showed no overall mortality benefit from ICDs in nonischemic DCM with LVEF ≤35%, despite reduced SCD incidence.
Mortality benefits were observed only in younger subgroups with lower competing risks of other causes of death.
items.reference: Cardiovascular Collapse Cardiac Arrest and Sudden Cardiac Death · TABLE 317-4 Implantable Cardioverter Defibrillator (ICD) Not Indicated | THE CHALLENGE OF SCD PREVENTION (FIG. 317-3)

items.subtopic: TABLE 317-4 Implantable Cardioverter Defibrillator (ICD) Not Indicated | THE CHALLENGE OF SCD PREVENTION (FIG. 317-3)
items.title: Situations where ICD is not indicated despite arrhythmias
items.content: An ICD should not be implanted in patients with ventricular tachyarrhythmias due to completely reversible disorders without structural heart disease, such as electrolyte imbalances or drug effects. Additionally, it's not indicated for syncope of undetermined cause if there are no inducible ventricular tachyarrhythmias and no structural heart disease.
keyPoints: ICD is not indicated for reversible causes of VT/VF without structural heart disease (e.g., electrolyte imbalance, drugs).
Syncope with undetermined cause should not lead to ICD placement if there are no inducible ventricular tachyarrhythmias and no structural heart disease.
ICD is also not indicated for incessant VT/VF or patients with significant psychiatric illnesses that may be aggravated by the device.
items.reference: Cardiovascular Collapse Cardiac Arrest and Sudden Cardiac Death · TABLE 317-4 Implantable Cardioverter Defibrillator (ICD) Not Indicated | THE CHALLENGE OF SCD PREVENTION (FIG. 317-3)

items.question: Which of the following is NOT a cause of cardiovascular collapse?
items.explanation: Chronic hypertension is not listed as a cause of cardiovascular collapse in the passage.

items.question: What percentage of sudden cardiac arrest victims survive to be discharged from the hospital?
items.explanation: The passage states that fewer than 10% of sudden cardiac arrest victims survive to be discharged from the hospital.
items.reference: Cardiovascular Collapse Cardiac Arrest and Sudden Cardiac Death · Cardiovascular Collapse, Cardiac Arrest, and Sudden Cardiac Death | OVERVIEW AND DEFINITIONS (SEE TABLE 317-1)

items.subtopic: Cardiovascular Collapse, Cardiac Arrest, and Sudden Cardiac Death | OVERVIEW AND DEFINITIONS (SEE TABLE 317-1)
items.question: Sudden cardiac death is often presumed to have a cardiovascular cause when:
options: There is evidence of a traumatic cause
The patient dies prior to hospital admission without evidence for a noncardiac cause
items.explanation: If the patient does not survive a sudden cardiac arrest and there is no evidence for a noncardiac cause, a cardiovascular cause is often presumed.
items.reference: Cardiovascular Collapse Cardiac Arrest and Sudden Cardiac Death · Cardiovascular Collapse, Cardiac Arrest, and Sudden Cardiac Death | OVERVIEW AND DEFINITIONS (SEE TABLE 317-1)

items.question: Which geographic region has the highest rate of out-of-hospital cardiac arrest per 100,000 person-years?
items.explanation: Australia has the highest rate at 111.9 per 100,000 person-years.
items.reference: Cardiovascular Collapse Cardiac Arrest and Sudden Cardiac Death · DEMOGRAPHICS

items.question: Which group is more likely to present with pulseless electrical activity (PEA) during a sudden cardiac death?
items.explanation: Women are more likely to present with PEA compared to men.
items.reference: Cardiovascular Collapse Cardiac Arrest and Sudden Cardiac Death · DEMOGRAPHICS

items.question: What is the estimated societal burden of premature death due to SCD in women, measured in years of potential life lost?
items.explanation: The societal burden for women is estimated at 1.3 million years.
items.reference: Cardiovascular Collapse Cardiac Arrest and Sudden Cardiac Death · DEMOGRAPHICS

items.question: Which condition is particularly associated with an increased risk of sudden cardiac death (SCD) in patients who do not yet have overt coronary heart disease (CHD)?
items.explanation: Smoking markedly elevates the risk of SCD, and cessation lowers this risk especially in individuals without overt CHD.
items.reference: Cardiovascular Collapse Cardiac Arrest and Sudden Cardiac Death · RISK FACTORS (SEE FIG. 317-1)

items.question: Which dietary component is associated with a lower risk of sudden cardiac death according to observational studies?
options: Polyunsaturated fatty acids, particularly n-3 fatty acids
items.explanation: Higher intakes of polyunsaturated fatty acids, especially n-3 fatty acids, are associated with lower SCD risks.
items.reference: Cardiovascular Collapse Cardiac Arrest and Sudden Cardiac Death · RISK FACTORS (SEE FIG. 317-1)

items.question: What is a significant risk factor for sudden cardiac death in black populations due to its higher prevalence?
options: Hypertension and resultant left ventricular hypertrophy (LVH)
items.explanation: Hypertension and resultant LVH are particularly important markers of SCD risk in blacks.
items.reference: Cardiovascular Collapse Cardiac Arrest and Sudden Cardiac Death · RISK FACTORS (SEE FIG. 317-1)

items.question: Which factor is most commonly associated with sudden cardiac death due to cardiac causes in white men?
items.explanation: Sudden death due to cardiac causes is most commonly due to coronary artery disease (CAD) in white men.
items.reference: Cardiovascular Collapse Cardiac Arrest and Sudden Cardiac Death · PRECIPITATING FACTORS | UNDERLYING HEART DISEASE (FIG. 317-1)

items.question: In which locations are sudden cardiac arrest arrests more likely to occur?
items.explanation: SCA arrests are more likely to occur in certain locations, with notable clustering around train stations, airports, and other public places.
items.reference: Cardiovascular Collapse Cardiac Arrest and Sudden Cardiac Death · PRECIPITATING FACTORS | UNDERLYING HEART DISEASE (FIG. 317-1)

items.question: What is the common thread among precipitating factors for sudden cardiac death?
items.explanation: The common thread among these precipitating factors is likely heightened autonomic tone.
items.reference: Cardiovascular Collapse Cardiac Arrest and Sudden Cardiac Death · PRECIPITATING FACTORS | UNDERLYING HEART DISEASE (FIG. 317-1)

items.question: What is the most common initial rhythm found at the scene of out-of-hospital cardiac arrests in public locations?
items.explanation: VF predominates in public locations due to a shorter time frame between witnessed arrest and EMS arrival.
items.reference: Cardiovascular Collapse Cardiac Arrest and Sudden Cardiac Death · CARDIAC RHYTHMS AND SUDDEN DEATH

items.question: Which of the following has been proposed as an explanation for the decrease in VF incidence?
options: Decreased use of beta blockers in CHD
Increased use of implantable cardioverter defibrillators (ICD)
items.explanation: The increased use of ICDs in high-risk patients is one proposed explanation for the decrease in VF incidence.
items.reference: Cardiovascular Collapse Cardiac Arrest and Sudden Cardiac Death · CARDIAC RHYTHMS AND SUDDEN DEATH

items.question: What trend has been observed regarding the proportion of cardiac arrests found in PEA over recent years?
options: Decrease due to improved cardiovascular health
Increase suggesting more abrupt hemodynamic collapse without preceding arrhythmia
Shift towards more frequent occurrences of sinus tachycardia
items.explanation: There has been an increase in PEA incidence, suggesting a rise in SCD due to abrupt hemodynamic collapse without preceding arrhythmia.
items.reference: Cardiovascular Collapse Cardiac Arrest and Sudden Cardiac Death · CARDIAC RHYTHMS AND SUDDEN DEATH

items.question: Which mechanism is most commonly associated with sudden cardiac death (SCD) following an acute myocardial infarction (MI)?
options: Polymorphic ventricular tachycardia (VT) and ventricular fibrillation (VF)
Severe bradyarrhythmias such as heart block
Pulseless electrical activity (PEA) due to a massive MI
items.explanation: Polymorphic VT and VF are the most common mechanisms leading to SCD following an acute MI.
items.reference: Cardiovascular Collapse Cardiac Arrest and Sudden Cardiac Death · DISEASE-SPECIFIC MECHANISMS

items.question: What is a primary mechanism of sudden cardiac death (SCD) in patients with nonischemic cardiomyopathies?
options: Pulseless electrical activity due to fluid overload
items.explanation: In nonischemic cardiomyopathies, scar-mediated reentrant VT is a primary mechanism of SCD.
items.reference: Cardiovascular Collapse Cardiac Arrest and Sudden Cardiac Death · DISEASE-SPECIFIC MECHANISMS

items.question: Which condition can lead to ventricular fibrillation (VF) in the absence of structural heart disease?
items.explanation: Inherited ion channel abnormalities, such as long QT syndrome, can lead to VF in the absence of structural heart disease.
items.reference: Cardiovascular Collapse Cardiac Arrest and Sudden Cardiac Death · DISEASE-SPECIFIC MECHANISMS

items.question: Which component of the 'out-of-hospital chain of survival' emphasizes rapid initiation with an emphasis on chest compressions?
options: Initial evaluation and recognition of SCA
items.explanation: The 'out-of-hospital chain of survival' includes rapid initiation of CPR with an emphasis on chest compressions.
items.reference: Cardiovascular Collapse Cardiac Arrest and Sudden Cardiac Death · MANAGEMENT OF CARDIAC ARREST

items.question: What is the initial goal of resuscitation in cardiac arrest management?
options: Achieve defibrillation as quickly as possible
items.explanation: The initial goal of resuscitation is to achieve the return of spontaneous circulation (ROSC).
items.reference: Cardiovascular Collapse Cardiac Arrest and Sudden Cardiac Death · MANAGEMENT OF CARDIAC ARREST

items.question: Which factor has been shown to contribute to reduced survival rates from out-of-hospital cardiac arrest in black and Hispanic populations in the United States?
options: Lack of access to advanced life support
items.explanation: Multiple studies have pointed to socioeconomic disparities in the administration of CPR and application of AEDs contributing to reduced survival rates.
items.reference: Cardiovascular Collapse Cardiac Arrest and Sudden Cardiac Death · MANAGEMENT OF CARDIAC ARREST

items.subtopic: INITIAL EVALUATION AND INITIATION OF CPR | RHYTHM-BASED MANAGEMENT (SEE FIG. 317-2)
items.question: What should a rescuer do if they suspect airway obstruction during the initial evaluation of a victim?
options: Perform the Heimlich maneuver to dislodge the obstructing body.
Administer two breaths for every 30 compressions.
Call or ask someone else to call their local emergency number.
items.explanation: The Heimlich maneuver may be used if airway obstruction is suspected.
items.reference: Cardiovascular Collapse Cardiac Arrest and Sudden Cardiac Death · INITIAL EVALUATION AND INITIATION OF CPR | RHYTHM-BASED MANAGEMENT (SEE FIG. 317-2)

items.subtopic: INITIAL EVALUATION AND INITIATION OF CPR | RHYTHM-BASED MANAGEMENT (SEE FIG. 317-2)
items.question: How long should a trained healthcare provider take to check for a pulse during initial evaluation?
items.explanation: Checking for a pulse should not take longer than 10 seconds to avoid delaying chest compressions.
items.reference: Cardiovascular Collapse Cardiac Arrest and Sudden Cardiac Death · INITIAL EVALUATION AND INITIATION OF CPR | RHYTHM-BASED MANAGEMENT (SEE FIG. 317-2)

items.subtopic: INITIAL EVALUATION AND INITIATION OF CPR | RHYTHM-BASED MANAGEMENT (SEE FIG. 317-2)
items.question: What is the recommended depth for chest compressions during CPR?
items.explanation: Chest compressions should depress the sternum by 5 cm (2 inches).
items.reference: Cardiovascular Collapse Cardiac Arrest and Sudden Cardiac Death · INITIAL EVALUATION AND INITIATION OF CPR | RHYTHM-BASED MANAGEMENT (SEE FIG. 317-2)

items.subtopic: INITIAL EVALUATION AND INITIATION OF CPR | RHYTHM-BASED MANAGEMENT (SEE FIG. 317-2)
items.question: What is the advised action if a second rescuer is present during cardiac arrest?
options: They should administer two breaths for every 30 compressions.
They should seek out an AED.
They should call or ask someone else to call their local emergency number.
items.explanation: If a second rescuer is present, they should be sent to seek out an AED.
items.reference: Cardiovascular Collapse Cardiac Arrest and Sudden Cardiac Death · INITIAL EVALUATION AND INITIATION OF CPR | RHYTHM-BASED MANAGEMENT (SEE FIG. 317-2)

items.question: Which intervention is recommended for all cardiac arrest patients who remain comatose after ROSC?
items.explanation: Therapeutic hypothermia is recommended for all comatose cardiac arrest patients post-ROSC to improve survival and neurologic recovery.
items.reference: Cardiovascular Collapse Cardiac Arrest and Sudden Cardiac Death · POSTCARDIAC ARREST ACUTE MANAGEMENT

items.question: What is the target systolic blood pressure following resuscitation?
items.explanation: Maintaining a systolic blood pressure >90 mmHg is desirable following resuscitation.
items.reference: Cardiovascular Collapse Cardiac Arrest and Sudden Cardiac Death · POSTCARDIAC ARREST ACUTE MANAGEMENT

items.question: Which factor does NOT influence the decision for urgent angiography in patients without ST-segment elevation?
options: Out-of-hospital cardiac arrest due to VT/VF with no ECG evidence of ST-segment elevation
items.explanation: Emergent angiography has not been found to result in better outcomes for patients presenting with out-of-hospital cardiac arrest due to VT/VF without ST-segment elevation.
items.reference: Cardiovascular Collapse Cardiac Arrest and Sudden Cardiac Death · POSTCARDIAC ARREST ACUTE MANAGEMENT

items.question: Which therapy was NOT shown to be beneficial for postarrest outcomes?
items.explanation: Corticosteroids have not been shown to improve postarrest outcomes.
items.reference: Cardiovascular Collapse Cardiac Arrest and Sudden Cardiac Death · POSTCARDIAC ARREST ACUTE MANAGEMENT

items.subtopic: LONG-TERM MANAGEMENT AFTER SURVIVAL OF OUT-OF-HOSPITAL CARDIAC ARREST
items.question: Which imaging modality is recommended to assess for prior myocardial infarction, valvular disease, and nonischemic cardiomyopathies in patients who survive an out-of-hospital cardiac arrest?
options: Echocardiography or cardiac magnetic resonance imaging (MRI)
items.explanation: Echocardiography and/or cardiac MRI are used to assess for prior MI, valvular disease, and nonischemic cardiomyopathies.
items.reference: Cardiovascular Collapse Cardiac Arrest and Sudden Cardiac Death · LONG-TERM MANAGEMENT AFTER SURVIVAL OF OUT-OF-HOSPITAL CARDIAC ARREST

items.subtopic: LONG-TERM MANAGEMENT AFTER SURVIVAL OF OUT-OF-HOSPITAL CARDIAC ARREST
items.question: In patients with a shockable rhythm at arrest that is not due to a transient reversible cause, what device should be considered for secondary prevention of sudden cardiac arrest/sudden cardiac death?
items.explanation: An ICD is recommended for secondary prevention in patients with shockable rhythms at arrest without a transient reversible cause.
items.reference: Cardiovascular Collapse Cardiac Arrest and Sudden Cardiac Death · LONG-TERM MANAGEMENT AFTER SURVIVAL OF OUT-OF-HOSPITAL CARDIAC ARREST

items.subtopic: LONG-TERM MANAGEMENT AFTER SURVIVAL OF OUT-OF-HOSPITAL CARDIAC ARREST
items.question: What is the recommendation regarding ICD implantation for patients who experience VF within 48 hours of an acute myocardial infarction?
options: ICD is generally not required due to similar risk as those without cardiac arrest.
ICD is only recommended if LVEF is <35%.
ICD implantation depends solely on patient preference.
items.explanation: Patients with VF within the first 48 hours of an acute MI generally do not require an ICD due to similar risks as those who did not have a cardiac arrest.
items.reference: Cardiovascular Collapse Cardiac Arrest and Sudden Cardiac Death · LONG-TERM MANAGEMENT AFTER SURVIVAL OF OUT-OF-HOSPITAL CARDIAC ARREST

items.subtopic: LONG-TERM MANAGEMENT AFTER SURVIVAL OF OUT-OF-HOSPITAL CARDIAC ARREST
items.question: Which patients are typically considered for ICD implantation after surviving an out-of-hospital cardiac arrest due to VT or VF?
options: Patients with reversible causes like hyperkalemia.
Patients with structural heart disease such as hypertrophic cardiomyopathy.
All patients regardless of underlying condition.
items.explanation: ICD is recommended for cardiac arrest due to VT or VF, particularly when structural heart disease like hypertrophic cardiomyopathy is present.
items.reference: Cardiovascular Collapse Cardiac Arrest and Sudden Cardiac Death · LONG-TERM MANAGEMENT AFTER SURVIVAL OF OUT-OF-HOSPITAL CARDIAC ARREST

items.subtopic: PREVENTION OF SCD | SCD RISK STRATIFICATION
items.question: Which factor contributes to a lower likelihood of successful resuscitation in patients who suffer cardiac arrest at home?
options: Longer EMS response times and less likely VF
items.explanation: Patients at home have longer EMS response times and are much less likely to be found in VF, reducing the chance of successful resuscitation.
items.reference: Cardiovascular Collapse Cardiac Arrest and Sudden Cardiac Death · PREVENTION OF SCD | SCD RISK STRATIFICATION

items.subtopic: PREVENTION OF SCD | SCD RISK STRATIFICATION
items.question: What is considered the best validated marker for increased risk of sudden cardiac death (SCD) in patients with structural heart disease?
options: Sustained VT induced at EP study
items.explanation: Depressed left ventricular function is the best validated marker for increased risk of SCD in patients with structural heart disease.
items.reference: Cardiovascular Collapse Cardiac Arrest and Sudden Cardiac Death · PREVENTION OF SCD | SCD RISK STRATIFICATION

items.subtopic: PREVENTION OF SCD | SCD RISK STRATIFICATION
items.question: Which statement about sustained VT at EP study is true?
options: It has high sensitivity when LV function is significantly reduced.
It identifies individuals at higher risk of non-sudden cardiac arrest (non-SCA).
It broadly predicts death from cardiovascular causes.
It identifies individuals at a higher risk of SCA versus non-SCA in certain subsets.
items.explanation: Sustained VT at EP study identifies individuals at a higher risk of SCA versus non-SCA in certain subsets, but its sensitivity is inadequate when LV function is significantly reduced.
items.reference: Cardiovascular Collapse Cardiac Arrest and Sudden Cardiac Death · PREVENTION OF SCD | SCD RISK STRATIFICATION

items.subtopic: ☑ PREVENTIVE THERAPIES FOR SCD IN HIGH-RISK POPULATIONS
items.question: Which medication class is recommended for reducing the risk of sudden cardiac death (SCD) in patients with ischemic and nonischemic cardiomyopathy?
items.explanation: Beta-adrenergic blockers have been demonstrated to reduce SCD risk in patients with ischemic and nonischemic cardiomyopathy.
items.reference: Cardiovascular Collapse Cardiac Arrest and Sudden Cardiac Death · ☑ PREVENTIVE THERAPIES FOR SCD IN HIGH-RISK POPULATIONS

items.subtopic: ☑ PREVENTIVE THERAPIES FOR SCD IN HIGH-RISK POPULATIONS
items.question: In which patient group is an ICD indicated for primary prevention of sudden death after a myocardial infarction (MI) more than 40 days ago?
options: Patients with class II–III NYHA HF and LVEF <35%
Patients with class I NYHA functional status and LVEF >30%
Patients within 90 days of myocardial revascularization
items.explanation: ICDs are indicated for primary prevention in patients with a history of MI >40 days ago, class II–III NYHA HF and LVEF <35%.
items.reference: Cardiovascular Collapse Cardiac Arrest and Sudden Cardiac Death · ☑ PREVENTIVE THERAPIES FOR SCD IN HIGH-RISK POPULATIONS

items.subtopic: ☑ PREVENTIVE THERAPIES FOR SCD IN HIGH-RISK POPULATIONS
items.question: Which imaging finding has been incorporated into ICD implantation guidelines due to its association with a risk of sudden death in patients with cardiac sarcoidosis and hypertrophic cardiomyopathy?
items.explanation: The presence of late gadolinium enhancement (LGE) on MRI is a risk factor for sudden death in patients with cardiac sarcoidosis and hypertrophic cardiomyopathy.
items.reference: Cardiovascular Collapse Cardiac Arrest and Sudden Cardiac Death · ☑ PREVENTIVE THERAPIES FOR SCD IN HIGH-RISK POPULATIONS

items.subtopic: TABLE 317-4 Implantable Cardioverter Defibrillator (ICD) Not Indicated | THE CHALLENGE OF SCD PREVENTION (FIG. 317-3)
items.question: Which of the following patients should not receive an ICD for primary prevention?
options: Patients with a reasonable expectation of survival and acceptable functional status for at least 1 year.
Patients who do not have a reasonable expectation of survival with an acceptable functional status for at least 1 year, even if they meet ICD implantation criteria.
Patients with incessant VT or VF.
Patients with significant psychiatric illnesses that may be aggravated by device implantation.
items.explanation: ICDs are not indicated in patients without a reasonable expectation of survival with acceptable functional status for at least 1 year.
items.reference: Cardiovascular Collapse Cardiac Arrest and Sudden Cardiac Death · TABLE 317-4 Implantable Cardioverter Defibrillator (ICD) Not Indicated | THE CHALLENGE OF SCD PREVENTION (FIG. 317-3)

items.subtopic: TABLE 317-4 Implantable Cardioverter Defibrillator (ICD) Not Indicated | THE CHALLENGE OF SCD PREVENTION (FIG. 317-3)
items.question: In which scenario is an ICD not indicated despite a significant risk of sudden cardiac death?
options: VF or VT amenable to surgical or catheter ablation in patients without other disease predisposing to sudden cardiac arrest.
Patients with ventricular tachyarrhythmias due to irreversible structural heart disease.
Patients with incessant VT or VF.
Patients with drug-refractory New York Heart Association class IV congestive heart failure.
items.explanation: ICDs are not indicated if VF or VT is amenable to surgical or catheter ablation in patients without other disease predisposing to sudden cardiac arrest.
items.reference: Cardiovascular Collapse Cardiac Arrest and Sudden Cardiac Death · TABLE 317-4 Implantable Cardioverter Defibrillator (ICD) Not Indicated | THE CHALLENGE OF SCD PREVENTION (FIG. 317-3)

items.subtopic: TABLE 317-4 Implantable Cardioverter Defibrillator (ICD) Not Indicated | THE CHALLENGE OF SCD PREVENTION (FIG. 317-3)
items.question: Which patient group is not a candidate for ICD placement due to the nature of their ventricular tachyarrhythmias?
options: Patients with ventricular tachyarrhythmias due to irreversible structural heart disease.
Patients with ventricular tachyarrhythmias due to completely reversible disorders in the absence of structural heart disease.
Patients with incessant VT or VF.
items.explanation: ICDs are not indicated for patients with ventricular tachyarrhythmias due to a completely reversible disorder in the absence of structural heart disease.
items.reference: Cardiovascular Collapse Cardiac Arrest and Sudden Cardiac Death · TABLE 317-4 Implantable Cardioverter Defibrillator (ICD) Not Indicated | THE CHALLENGE OF SCD PREVENTION (FIG. 317-3)

items.subtopic: Cardiovascular Collapse, Cardiac Arrest, and Sudden Cardiac Death | OVERVIEW AND DEFINITIONS (SEE TABLE 317-1)
items.statement: Most sudden cardiac arrests occur within the hospital.
items.explanation: The passage states that most sudden cardiac arrests occur outside the hospital.
items.reference: Cardiovascular Collapse Cardiac Arrest and Sudden Cardiac Death · Cardiovascular Collapse, Cardiac Arrest, and Sudden Cardiac Death | OVERVIEW AND DEFINITIONS (SEE TABLE 317-1)

items.subtopic: Cardiovascular Collapse, Cardiac Arrest, and Sudden Cardiac Death | OVERVIEW AND DEFINITIONS (SEE TABLE 317-1)
items.statement: Sudden cardiac death includes deaths within 30 days after a resuscitated cardiac arrest.
items.explanation: The passage mentions that deaths occurring during hospitalization or within 30 days after resuscitated cardiac arrest are usually counted as sudden cardiac deaths.
items.reference: Cardiovascular Collapse Cardiac Arrest and Sudden Cardiac Death · Cardiovascular Collapse, Cardiac Arrest, and Sudden Cardiac Death | OVERVIEW AND DEFINITIONS (SEE TABLE 317-1)

items.statement: SCD rates have declined more steeply than the rates for CHD in general.
items.explanation: SCD rates have not declined as steeply as rates for CHD.
items.reference: Cardiovascular Collapse Cardiac Arrest and Sudden Cardiac Death · DEMOGRAPHICS

items.statement: Hispanic individuals have lower rates of SCD despite having a higher prevalence of cardiac risk factors.
items.explanation: Hispanic ethnicity is associated with lower rates of SCD.
items.reference: Cardiovascular Collapse Cardiac Arrest and Sudden Cardiac Death · DEMOGRAPHICS

items.statement: A history of sudden cardiac death in a first-degree relative is associated with an increased risk for acute myocardial infarction.
items.explanation: A family history of SCD increases the risk for SCD and VF during acute MI, but not for acute MI itself.
items.reference: Cardiovascular Collapse Cardiac Arrest and Sudden Cardiac Death · RISK FACTORS (SEE FIG. 317-1)

items.statement: Obstructive sleep apnea is associated with an increased risk of sudden cardiac death.
items.explanation: Obstructive sleep apnea increases SCD risk, potentially due to hypoxia-induced cardiac arrest.
items.reference: Cardiovascular Collapse Cardiac Arrest and Sudden Cardiac Death · RISK FACTORS (SEE FIG. 317-1)

items.statement: Sudden cardiac death rates are consistently higher during the summer in both hemispheres.
items.explanation: SCD rates peak during winter in the northern hemisphere and summer in the southern hemisphere.
items.reference: Cardiovascular Collapse Cardiac Arrest and Sudden Cardiac Death · PRECIPITATING FACTORS | UNDERLYING HEART DISEASE (FIG. 317-1)

items.statement: Habitual exercise completely eliminates the acute risk of exertion-associated sudden cardiac death.
items.explanation: Habitual exercise and training lower this acute risk but do not eliminate it entirely.
items.reference: Cardiovascular Collapse Cardiac Arrest and Sudden Cardiac Death · PRECIPITATING FACTORS | UNDERLYING HEART DISEASE (FIG. 317-1)

items.statement: The rhythm at the time of EMS arrival always reflects the initial rhythm that precipitated sudden cardiac arrest.
items.explanation: Arrhythmias can change once hemodynamic collapse occurs, so the rhythm at EMS arrival may not reflect the initial rhythm.
items.reference: Cardiovascular Collapse Cardiac Arrest and Sudden Cardiac Death · CARDIAC RHYTHMS AND SUDDEN DEATH

items.statement: The aging population and increased prevalence of severe comorbidities are proposed explanations for changes in PEA versus VF incidence.
items.explanation: These factors contribute to a higher likelihood of arrests due to acute precipitants leading to PEA.
items.reference: Cardiovascular Collapse Cardiac Arrest and Sudden Cardiac Death · CARDIAC RHYTHMS AND SUDDEN DEATH

items.statement: The risk of sudden cardiac death (SCD) is highest within the first month after an acute myocardial infarction.
items.explanation: The risk of SCD is elevated up to 10-fold, with the highest absolute rates in the first 30 days post-MI.
items.reference: Cardiovascular Collapse Cardiac Arrest and Sudden Cardiac Death · DISEASE-SPECIFIC MECHANISMS

items.statement: Pulseless electrical activity (PEA) can result from drug toxicities that prolong the QT interval.
items.explanation: Drug toxicities that prolong the QT interval typically cause polymorphic VT, not PEA.
items.reference: Cardiovascular Collapse Cardiac Arrest and Sudden Cardiac Death · DISEASE-SPECIFIC MECHANISMS

items.statement: Survival rates for out-of-hospital cardiac arrest are higher for patients found in VT or VF.
items.explanation: Survival rates can approach 30% in some regions for patients found in VT or VF.
items.reference: Cardiovascular Collapse Cardiac Arrest and Sudden Cardiac Death · MANAGEMENT OF CARDIAC ARREST

items.statement: The success of resuscitation is independent of the time between collapse and initiation of resuscitation.
items.explanation: Success is strongly related to the time between collapse and initiation of resuscitation, decreasing markedly after 5 minutes.
items.reference: Cardiovascular Collapse Cardiac Arrest and Sudden Cardiac Death · MANAGEMENT OF CARDIAC ARREST

items.subtopic: INITIAL EVALUATION AND INITIATION OF CPR | RHYTHM-BASED MANAGEMENT (SEE FIG. 317-2)
items.statement: Gasping respirations during SCA should be interpreted as effective breathing.
items.explanation: Gasping respirations may be misinterpreted as breathing but are not considered effective.
items.reference: Cardiovascular Collapse Cardiac Arrest and Sudden Cardiac Death · INITIAL EVALUATION AND INITIATION OF CPR | RHYTHM-BASED MANAGEMENT (SEE FIG. 317-2)

items.subtopic: INITIAL EVALUATION AND INITIATION OF CPR | RHYTHM-BASED MANAGEMENT (SEE FIG. 317-2)
items.statement: Lay rescuers should perform hands-only CPR if they lack training.
items.explanation: Hands-only CPR is more likely to be effectively applied by lay rescuers without training.
items.reference: Cardiovascular Collapse Cardiac Arrest and Sudden Cardiac Death · INITIAL EVALUATION AND INITIATION OF CPR | RHYTHM-BASED MANAGEMENT (SEE FIG. 317-2)

items.statement: Therapeutic hypothermia should be initiated before hospital arrival.
items.explanation: Induction of hypothermia is recommended to start in the hospital, not before arrival.
items.reference: Cardiovascular Collapse Cardiac Arrest and Sudden Cardiac Death · POSTCARDIAC ARREST ACUTE MANAGEMENT

items.statement: Functional neurologic assessment for recovery should be deferred until at least 72 hours after return to normothermia.
items.explanation: Assessment is generally deferred for at least 72 hours post-return to normothermia.
items.reference: Cardiovascular Collapse Cardiac Arrest and Sudden Cardiac Death · POSTCARDIAC ARREST ACUTE MANAGEMENT

items.subtopic: LONG-TERM MANAGEMENT AFTER SURVIVAL OF OUT-OF-HOSPITAL CARDIAC ARREST
items.statement: ICD implantation early after myocardial infarction improves overall survival.
items.explanation: Early ICD implantation does not improve overall survival due to risks like recurrent ischemia or myocardial rupture.
items.reference: Cardiovascular Collapse Cardiac Arrest and Sudden Cardiac Death · LONG-TERM MANAGEMENT AFTER SURVIVAL OF OUT-OF-HOSPITAL CARDIAC ARREST

items.subtopic: LONG-TERM MANAGEMENT AFTER SURVIVAL OF OUT-OF-HOSPITAL CARDIAC ARREST
items.statement: Patients with cardiac arrest due to hyperkalemia usually require an ICD.
items.explanation: Hyperkalemia is a reversible cause, and patients do not usually need an ICD if it can be adequately addressed.
items.reference: Cardiovascular Collapse Cardiac Arrest and Sudden Cardiac Death · LONG-TERM MANAGEMENT AFTER SURVIVAL OF OUT-OF-HOSPITAL CARDIAC ARREST

items.subtopic: PREVENTION OF SCD | SCD RISK STRATIFICATION
items.statement: The majority of cardiac arrests occur in public places where AEDs and rapid defibrillation have the greatest impact.
items.explanation: Most cardiac arrests do not occur in public places; they often happen at home, reducing the impact of AEDs and rapid defibrillation.
items.reference: Cardiovascular Collapse Cardiac Arrest and Sudden Cardiac Death · PREVENTION OF SCD | SCD RISK STRATIFICATION

items.subtopic: PREVENTION OF SCD | SCD RISK STRATIFICATION
items.statement: Patients with a low LVEF are more likely to die from pump failure than sudden cardiac arrest.
items.explanation: Patients with the greatest degree of systolic heart failure and/or lowest LVEF, although at elevated risk for SCD, are more likely to die from pump failure.
items.reference: Cardiovascular Collapse Cardiac Arrest and Sudden Cardiac Death · PREVENTION OF SCD | SCD RISK STRATIFICATION

items.subtopic: ☑ PREVENTIVE THERAPIES FOR SCD IN HIGH-RISK POPULATIONS
items.statement: ICDs are beneficial when implanted within 40 days of an MI.
items.explanation: ICDs have not been found to be beneficial when implanted within 40 days of an MI.
items.reference: Cardiovascular Collapse Cardiac Arrest and Sudden Cardiac Death · ☑ PREVENTIVE THERAPIES FOR SCD IN HIGH-RISK POPULATIONS

items.subtopic: ☑ PREVENTIVE THERAPIES FOR SCD IN HIGH-RISK POPULATIONS
items.statement: Primary prevention ICDs are recommended for patients with nonischemic dilated cardiomyopathy (DCM) who have LVEF ≤35% and NYHA functional class II or III symptoms.
items.explanation: ICDs are indicated for primary prevention in high-risk patients with nonischemic DCM, LVEF ≤35%, and NYHA class II or III symptoms.
items.reference: Cardiovascular Collapse Cardiac Arrest and Sudden Cardiac Death · ☑ PREVENTIVE THERAPIES FOR SCD IN HIGH-RISK POPULATIONS

items.subtopic: TABLE 317-4 Implantable Cardioverter Defibrillator (ICD) Not Indicated | THE CHALLENGE OF SCD PREVENTION (FIG. 317-3)
items.statement: The DANISH trial demonstrated an overall mortality benefit of ICDs in patients with nonischemic dilated cardiomyopathy and LVEF ≤35%.
items.explanation: The DANISH trial did not demonstrate an overall mortality benefit despite a reduction in the incidence of SCD.
items.reference: Cardiovascular Collapse Cardiac Arrest and Sudden Cardiac Death · TABLE 317-4 Implantable Cardioverter Defibrillator (ICD) Not Indicated | THE CHALLENGE OF SCD PREVENTION (FIG. 317-3)

items.subtopic: TABLE 317-4 Implantable Cardioverter Defibrillator (ICD) Not Indicated | THE CHALLENGE OF SCD PREVENTION (FIG. 317-3)
items.statement: Patients with syncope of undetermined cause without inducible ventricular tachyarrhythmias and structural heart disease are candidates for ICD.
items.explanation: ICDs are not indicated in patients with syncope of undetermined cause without inducible ventricular tachyarrhythmias and without structural heart disease.
items.reference: Cardiovascular Collapse Cardiac Arrest and Sudden Cardiac Death · TABLE 317-4 Implantable Cardioverter Defibrillator (ICD) Not Indicated | THE CHALLENGE OF SCD PREVENTION (FIG. 317-3)

items.subtopic: Cardiovascular Collapse, Cardiac Arrest, and Sudden Cardiac Death | OVERVIEW AND DEFINITIONS (SEE TABLE 317-1)
items.assertion: Most countries have national surveillance systems for sudden cardiac death.
items.reason: The true incidence and frequency of sudden cardiac death can be accurately estimated.
items.explanation: The passage states that most countries, including the United States, do not have national surveillance systems or reporting requirements for sudden cardiac death.
items.reference: Cardiovascular Collapse Cardiac Arrest and Sudden Cardiac Death · Cardiovascular Collapse, Cardiac Arrest, and Sudden Cardiac Death | OVERVIEW AND DEFINITIONS (SEE TABLE 317-1)

items.assertion: The majority of exertion-associated sudden cardiac deaths occur in highly trained athletes.
items.reason: Exertion-associated SCDs are particularly tragic and highly publicized when they occur in highly trained athletes.
items.explanation: While exertion-associated SCDs in athletes are highly publicized, the majority actually occur in the general population.
items.reference: Cardiovascular Collapse Cardiac Arrest and Sudden Cardiac Death · PRECIPITATING FACTORS | UNDERLYING HEART DISEASE (FIG. 317-1)

items.assertion: The proportion of cardiac arrests found in VF has decreased since the 1970s.
items.reason: This decrease is due to factors such as lower CHD incidence and increased use of beta blockers and ICDs.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Cardiovascular Collapse Cardiac Arrest and Sudden Cardiac Death · CARDIAC RHYTHMS AND SUDDEN DEATH

items.assertion: The rhythm at the time of EMT arrival is a critical factor in determining outcomes.
items.reason: Outcomes are best for VT, worse for VF, and poor for PEA and asystole.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the rhythm at the time of EMT arrival is indeed a critical factor in determining outcomes.
items.reference: Cardiovascular Collapse Cardiac Arrest and Sudden Cardiac Death · MANAGEMENT OF CARDIAC ARREST

items.subtopic: INITIAL EVALUATION AND INITIATION OF CPR | RHYTHM-BASED MANAGEMENT (SEE FIG. 317-2)
items.assertion: Chest compressions should be resumed immediately after a defibrillator shock.
items.reason: Immediate resumption of chest compressions reduces end-organ ischemia.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Cardiovascular Collapse Cardiac Arrest and Sudden Cardiac Death · INITIAL EVALUATION AND INITIATION OF CPR | RHYTHM-BASED MANAGEMENT (SEE FIG. 317-2)

items.subtopic: INITIAL EVALUATION AND INITIATION OF CPR | RHYTHM-BASED MANAGEMENT (SEE FIG. 317-2)
items.assertion: Amiodarone is administered after successful defibrillation.
items.reason: Amiodarone prevents arrhythmia recurrence following a shock.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but amiodarone is used if VT/VF recurs after shocks, not necessarily immediately after successful defibrillation.
items.reference: Cardiovascular Collapse Cardiac Arrest and Sudden Cardiac Death · INITIAL EVALUATION AND INITIATION OF CPR | RHYTHM-BASED MANAGEMENT (SEE FIG. 317-2)

items.assertion: Hypothermia and sedation preclude reliable prognostication for neurologic recovery.
items.reason: Functional neurologic assessment is deferred until at least 72 hours after return to normothermia.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason explains why hypothermia precludes reliable prognostication.
items.reference: Cardiovascular Collapse Cardiac Arrest and Sudden Cardiac Death · POSTCARDIAC ARREST ACUTE MANAGEMENT

items.assertion: The probability of successful neurologic recovery decreases rapidly with time from collapse to ROSC.
items.reason: Therapeutic hypothermia has no impact on the likelihood of survival and neurologic recovery.
items.explanation: While the assertion is true, therapeutic hypothermia does improve the likelihood of survival and neurologic recovery.
items.reference: Cardiovascular Collapse Cardiac Arrest and Sudden Cardiac Death · POSTCARDIAC ARREST ACUTE MANAGEMENT

items.subtopic: LONG-TERM MANAGEMENT AFTER SURVIVAL OF OUT-OF-HOSPITAL CARDIAC ARREST
items.assertion: Patients with large infarctions and acutely depressed LVEF have an increased risk for life-threatening ventricular arrhythmias.
items.reason: The presence of reentry in the infarct scar contributes to this increased risk.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Cardiovascular Collapse Cardiac Arrest and Sudden Cardiac Death · LONG-TERM MANAGEMENT AFTER SURVIVAL OF OUT-OF-HOSPITAL CARDIAC ARREST

items.subtopic: LONG-TERM MANAGEMENT AFTER SURVIVAL OF OUT-OF-HOSPITAL CARDIAC ARREST
items.assertion: ICDs should be implanted in all patients with end-stage heart disease.
items.reason: ICDs can alter the course of terminal, end-stage heart disease.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because ICDs do not alter the course of terminal, end-stage heart disease; the reason is also false.
items.reference: Cardiovascular Collapse Cardiac Arrest and Sudden Cardiac Death · LONG-TERM MANAGEMENT AFTER SURVIVAL OF OUT-OF-HOSPITAL CARDIAC ARREST

items.subtopic: ☑ PREVENTIVE THERAPIES FOR SCD IN HIGH-RISK POPULATIONS
items.assertion: ICDs are recommended for primary prevention in patients with arrhythmogenic right ventricular dysplasia.
items.reason: These patients are at high risk of sudden cardiac death due to their condition.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why ICDs are recommended for these patients.
items.reference: Cardiovascular Collapse Cardiac Arrest and Sudden Cardiac Death · ☑ PREVENTIVE THERAPIES FOR SCD IN HIGH-RISK POPULATIONS

items.subtopic: TABLE 317-4 Implantable Cardioverter Defibrillator (ICD) Not Indicated | THE CHALLENGE OF SCD PREVENTION (FIG. 317-3)
items.assertion: Patients with significant psychiatric illnesses may not be suitable candidates for ICD implantation.
items.reason: Significant psychiatric illnesses may be aggravated by device implantation or preclude systematic follow-up.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why patients with significant psychiatric illnesses may not be suitable for ICD implantation.
items.reference: Cardiovascular Collapse Cardiac Arrest and Sudden Cardiac Death · TABLE 317-4 Implantable Cardioverter Defibrillator (ICD) Not Indicated | THE CHALLENGE OF SCD PREVENTION (FIG. 317-3)
